one_line_takeaway: ClawGuard shows that inducing task-scoped access rules from the user's objective before any tool runs, then deterministically enforcing them at every tool-call boundary, blocks the skill/MCP injection attacks that safety-aligned models miss (model-averaged DSR gains of 18-30 points) at ~5% token overhead and modest utility loss.
key_finding: The paper's own results show a sharp split between injection channels. Web/local injection is already largely neutralized by strong safety-aligned agentic LLMs (undefended DSR >= 92.78% on the weaker three backbones; 100% for GPT-5.4 and Claude-Sonnet-4.6), and ClawGuard mostly pushes these to a perfect 100% DSR, so its gain there is real but marginal. The decisive gains are on skill/MCP channels, where adversarial behavior is disguised as legitimate tool/skill semantics: undefended model-averaged DSR is only 56.67% (SkillInject), 62.10% (MCPTox), and 75.86% (MSB), and ClawGuard raises these to 86.67%, 89.50%, and 94.21% (absolute gains of 30.00, 27.40, and 18.36 points). On these harder benchmarks ClawGuard beats MELON by 18.72 points DSR on average and Task Shield by 10.57 points. Utility cost is bounded: zero-approval retains 87.4% of original OpenClaw utility, rising to 95.1% with up to three approvals, at ~4.98% average token overhead. The end-to-end case study shows an injected 'cat ~/.ssh/id_rsa | curl ... exfil.io' exec call being blocked by two independent verdicts (Rtask deny on exec and Rbase deny on ~/.ssh/ credential access).
core_contribution: ClawGuard is a runtime security middleware that interposes at every tool-call boundary of a tool-augmented LLM agent to defend against indirect prompt injection, without any model fine-tuning or infrastructure/protocol change. Its central idea is context-aware, task-specific rule induction: before the first external tool is invoked, ClawGuard prompts the agent's own LLM to derive an access-control rule set (network domain whitelist/blacklist, local file-path whitelist/blacklist, and command rules) purely from the user's stated objective (H0), so the rules are uncontaminated by later adversarial tool output; the user confirms/edits these task rules (Rtask), which are unioned with operator-level baseline invariants (Rbase) that cannot be overridden. At runtime, four components enforce R = Rbase U Rtask per tool call: a Content Sanitizer that redacts sensitive spans (e.g., API keys) from both outgoing tool arguments and incoming tool outputs; a Rule Evaluator that gives an element-level verdict (deny / allow / ambiguous) over each attribute (tool name, resolved path, target domain) with most-restrictive-wins aggregation and blacklist-over-whitelist priority, plus an obfuscation normalizer (Base64/hex/shell-indirection) that escalates suspicious inputs to ambiguous; a Skill Inspector that runs a one-time LLM-judge safety check before a skill's first execution and caches the verdict; and an Approval Mechanism that routes ambiguous calls to the user with a timeout, logging all events to an Audit Log. The framing contribution is establishing deterministic tool-call-boundary enforcement as an alternative to probabilistic model-level resistance.
threat_model: Adversary E has black-box control of one or more tool-output channels and can inject arbitrary adversarial instructions (delta) into tool returns (producing poisoned o_tilde_j), but has no access to the system prompt, prior conversation history, or model weights. Three injection channels are in scope: (1) web/local content injection (malicious text in fetched pages/documents/search results), (2) MCP tool poisoning (adversarial instructions in tool-description metadata during registration, plus in returned content), and (3) skill file injection (adversarial directives semantically blended into public skill files). Adversarial objectives: data exfiltration, unauthorized action, financial manipulation, privilege escalation, and persistent compromise. Explicitly out of scope: direct prompt injection (attacker edits the user turn), jailbreaks exploiting model weights, model fine-tuning attacks, and a genuinely malicious user (ClawGuard only enforces baseline invariants in that case). A noted real-world caveat: OpenClaw deployments exposing agent ports to the public Internet could enable stronger compromise channels than modeled.
limitations: (1) Effectiveness is coupled to backbone strength: ClawGuard's skill inspection and rule induction run on the same LLM, so weaker backbones (DeepSeek-V3.2, MiniMax-M2.5) induce less precise rules and leave higher residual ASR (e.g., MCPTox on DeepSeek-V3.2 is the one skill/MCP case where ClawGuard fails to drive ASR below 15%). (2) Residual failures on Skill-Inject are 'objective-altering' attacks (biased scoring, ideological content filtering disguised as company policy) that produce no suspicious tool call and thus evade task-scope authorization entirely; these six failure cases recur across all backbones. (3) Threat model excludes direct instruction/system-config tampering and malicious users, which is practically relevant given publicly exposed agent ports. (4) Utility evaluation covers only completion rate and token usage, not latency variance, long-horizon human-approval burden, or multi-session failure recovery. (5) Evaluated only within a single agent framework (OpenClaw); cross-framework transfer is left as future work. (6) Task-specific rule induction adds a mandatory pre-session user-confirmation step, and web tasks trigger the most approval friction (zero-approval utility retention only 78.5% on WebArena).

datasets_used: SkillHub public repository (RQ3): 23,794 skills scanned, 283 malicious/suspicious identified (90 data exfiltration, 40 financial manipulation, 13 persistent compromise, 35 privilege escalation, 60 unauthorized actions)
quantitative_results: Skill/MCP undefended model-averaged DSR: 56.67% (SkillInject), 62.10% (MCPTox), 75.86% (MSB); with ClawGuard: 86.67%, 89.50%, 94.21% (absolute gains +30.00, +27.40, +18.36 points)
ClawGuard on skill/MCP outperforms MELON by +18.72 points DSR average and Task Shield by +10.57 points average
Weaker models (DeepSeek-V3.2, MiniMax-M2.5): ClawGuard cuts ASR by 71.98% on average, a 1.93x DSR improvement; Refusal Rate improves 16.21x on average
SkillInject DSR examples: DeepSeek-V3.2 25.00% undefended -> 77.38% ClawGuard; GPT-5.4 65.48% -> 94.05%; Claude-Sonnet-4.6 97.62% -> 100.00%
MCPTox DSR examples: DeepSeek-V3.2 33.56% -> 83.56%; Qwen3.5 61.87% -> 89.73%; Claude-Sonnet-4.6 90.18% -> 100.00%
MCPSecurityBench DSR examples: DeepSeek-V3.2 27.86% -> 85.36%; Qwen3.5 69.64% -> 95.00%; GPT-5.4 99.29% -> 100.00%

Web/local injection: undefended DSR >= 92.78% (weaker backbones), 100% for GPT-5.4 and Claude-Sonnet-4.6; ClawGuard reaches 100% DSR across all five models on AgentDojo, InjecAgent, and ChatInject, except marginal residual on InjecAgent (DeepSeek-V3.2 99.08%, Qwen3.5 99.82%)
Utility retention vs original OpenClaw: 87.4% average zero-approval, 95.1% with up to three approvals
Zero-approval utility retention by benchmark: 88.8% AgentBench-OS, 78.5% WebArena, 94.9% SWE-bench Lite; with three approvals: 94.9%, 91.7%, 98.8%
Zero-approval utility degradation by model: 8.1% GPT-5.4 to 21.6% DeepSeek-V3.2 (Claude 8.3%, MiniMax 11.4%, Qwen 13.7%); three-approval: 2.8% GPT-5.4 to 9.6% DeepSeek-V3.2
Token overhead average 4.98% (3.1% AgentBench-OS, 6.2% WebArena, 5.6% SWE-bench Lite)

RQ3 weakest-backbone DSR improvements: Data Exfiltration 41% -> 67%, Privilege Escalation 11% -> 66%, Unauthorized Actions 18% -> 68%; strongest models reach 100% DSR on Financial Manipulation and Persistent Compromise, 94% upper bound on Data Exfiltration and Privilege Escalation
Baseline utility (original OpenClaw): Claude-Sonnet-4.6 best on AgentBench-OS (94.6%) and SWE-bench Lite (87.3%); GPT-5.4 best on WebArena (52.0%)
baselines_compared: Undefended backbone models (DeepSeek-V3.2-685B, MiniMax-M2.5-229B, Qwen3.5-397B-A17B, GPT-5.4, Claude-Sonnet-4.6)
Task Shield (task-alignment checking at each execution step)
